metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify what `run_ensures()` does when a unit callable returns `None` (or any non-`EnsureOutcome`); and decide whether the gitattributes unit should re-raise OSError (letting the outer catch mark it `failed`) rather than swallowing it internally — the two error-handling layers conflict.
findings.finding: The plan asserts '_commit_gitattributes' uses a tree-check-then-commit pattern for its migration arm, but the existing migration arm (`_RETIRED_GITATTRIBUTES_LINES` stripping, lines 439-458) wraps its OSError in a log-and-continue that swallows the exception before the ensure wrapper sees it. The plan's ensure registry catches 'a raising unit' and marks it `failed`, but if the callable internally swallows OSError (as the existing migration arm does), the unit will return `None` instead of an `EnsureOutcome`, which the plan never specifies how to handle — the spec only defines the callable as `callable(tracker) -> EnsureOutcome`.
findings.location: What section / Migrate exactly the 5 re-init idempotent units; _commit_gitattributes lines 457-458 in init.py
findings.suggested_fix: Have the migrated gitattributes ensure unit re-raise on OSError (remove the internal swallow) so the outer `run_ensures()` catch-and-continue logic consistently handles all failures and marks the unit `failed`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for 'no other callers remain' in AC5, e.g.: `grep -rn '_ensure_env_id\|_migrate_gc_config\|_ensure_gc_config\|_ensure_merge_ours\|_ensure_gitattributes\|_ensure_gitignore' src/rebar/ | grep -v 'ensures.py'` must return empty, and state this (or an equivalent AST-based test) explicitly in the AC.
findings.finding: AC5 says 'Confirm no OTHER callers of the 5 helpers remain after removal (no double-execution)' but provides no concrete proving command (e.g., a grep or test assertion), leaving the no-double-execution claim unverifiable as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5: 'Confirm no OTHER callers of the 5 helpers remain after removal'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC5: 'Verified by: `grep -rn \'_ensure_env_id\\|_migrate_gc_config\\|_ensure_gc_config\\|_ensure_merge_ours_driver\\|_ensure_gitattributes\\|_ensure_gitignore\' src/rebar/ | grep -v ensures.py` returns no output.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the failure-logging protocol ('init_core logs any failed units (warning) and NEVER aborts') to explicitly cover the two `_init_via_symlink` `run_ensures()` call sites, or state that `run_ensures()` itself handles all logging so no call-site protocol is needed.
findings.finding: The failure-logging protocol for `run_ensures()` in `_init_via_symlink` is unspecified. The plan states 'init_core logs any failed units (warning) and NEVER aborts on an ensure failure' but makes no equivalent statement for the two `_init_via_symlink` call sites where `run_ensures()` replaces `_ensure_env_id`. The fallback (never abort, return 0) is implied by 'run_ensures() NEVER raises', but whether failed units in the symlink path are logged (and at what level) is a T8(d/b) gap.
findings.location: What section ('In `_init_via_symlink`, the two `_ensure_env_id` call sites...') / AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: '`run_ensures()` itself emits a WARNING log for each failed unit (so callers including `_init_via_symlink` need no additional logging logic beyond calling `run_ensures()`)' — this removes the ambiguity by making logging an internal responsibility of `run_ensures`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the log level for the LockTimeout whole-sweep no-op (e.g., 'logged at WARNING level with the tracker path and exception message') co-located with the no-op protocol description in the What section.
findings.finding: The log level for the LockTimeout whole-sweep no-op is unspecified. The plan says 'logged; the caller — init or MCP boot — continues, never aborts' but does not name a log level (WARNING, INFO, DEBUG). Per-unit failures are explicitly at 'warning' level, creating an inconsistency where the more severe event (entire sweep skipped vs. one unit failed) has a less-specified logging protocol.
findings.location: What section ('a write_lock acquisition failure (LockTimeout) is caught and treated like a whole-sweep no-op (logged)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'a LockTimeout whole-sweep no-op is logged at WARNING level (e.g., "ensure sweep skipped: could not acquire write lock on {tracker}") so operators can detect contention without aborting the caller.'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `_init_via_symlink` also logs a warning for any `failed` EnsureOutcome (matching the init_core contract), and add an AC assertion that both entry points emit a warning when a unit fails.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `init_core` logs failed ensure units as warnings, but the two `_init_via_symlink` replacement sites are given no equivalent logging requirement. A failed unit in either symlink-init branch produces a `failed` EnsureOutcome that is silently dropped from `.ensure-applied`, with no warning surfaced to the operator or caller.
findings.location: What section — `_init_via_symlink` wiring paragraph; AC line: 'init logs failures and never aborts' (which names only init_core)
findings.suggested_fix: In the `_init_via_symlink` wiring paragraph, add: 'After each `run_ensures(tracker)` call, log a warning for every EnsureOutcome with status `failed`, matching the init_core behaviour.' Update the AC line to read 'ALL init entry points (init_core and _init_via_symlink) log failures and never abort.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the id format rule for ensure units (e.g., 'non-empty lowercase kebab-case string matching `[a-z][a-z0-9-]*`') in the `ensures.py` definition section, and canonicalize 'merge-ours driver' to 'merge-ours-driver' (or document the space as intentional).
findings.finding: The `ensure-unit` id format/namespace is referenced but never defined. The plan names 5 ids in prose ('env-id', 'gc-config', 'merge-ours driver', 'gitattributes', 'gitignore') but gives no format rule — 'merge-ours driver' contains a space while the others are kebab-case, and no constraint (e.g., must be a non-empty alphanumeric-kebab string, no spaces) is stated. AC1's 'frozen expected set' test implicitly pins the ids, but an executor writing a new ensure unit has no rule for choosing a valid id.
findings.location: What section / AC1 / ensures.py (to be created)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the 'ordering is preserved' claim in the What section: specify that the ordering guarantee is that `run_ensures()` runs AFTER all non-migrated bootstrap steps (so the worktree is fully set up), not that the relative commit order of gitignore/gitattributes versus _commit_precommit is maintained (it changes); confirm this reordering is intentional and harmless.
findings.finding: The plan says 'ordering is preserved' when placing run_ensures() after the fresh-init bootstrap, but the relative ordering of two migrated units (gitignore, gitattributes) versus the non-migrated _commit_precommit changes: currently gitignore is committed at step 242 (before precommit at 244 and gitattributes at 245), but post-migration all three commit-units run together inside run_ensures() after _gen_local_files(246) — i.e., after precommit. While not a correctness problem (the three committed files are independent), the plan's claim that 'ordering is preserved' is misleading and could cause an implementer to infer that the within-bootstrap relative order is maintained when it is not.
findings.location: What section: 'placing run_ensures() AFTER the fresh-init bootstrap so ordering is preserved'; also AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'placing run_ensures() AFTER the fresh-init bootstrap so ordering is preserved' with 'placing run_ensures() AFTER the fresh-init bootstrap ensures the worktree is fully set up before convergence runs; note that on fresh-init the gitignore/gitattributes commits now occur after _commit_precommit rather than interleaved — this is intentional and correct since the three files are independent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale in the What section explaining WHY the store write lock is the correct primitive for run_ensures(): it serializes the check-then-act idempotency units against concurrent event_append and sync writers (which also hold write_lock), so a unit that checks-then-commits cannot race a concurrent git operation; and explain why LockTimeout is a whole-sweep no-op (matching sync.py's established pattern: ensures are best-effort convergence, not a hard requirement for the caller to succeed).
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for why the STORE write lock (lock.py write_lock) is the right locking primitive for run_ensures() — specifically why it must be the same lock used by event_append/sync rather than, say, a dedicated ensures-lock or the existing init mkdir-lock. The 'Why' section explains the feature rationale but not the locking mechanism choice. This leaves an implementer without a stated reason why LockTimeout inside run_ensures() should be a silent whole-sweep no-op rather than, e.g., a retry or a fallback to lockless execution.
findings.location: What section: 'run_ensures(tracker) runs ALL units unconditionally under the STORE write lock (_store/lock.py write_lock — init_core does NOT currently hold it; this is a new dependency)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the registry container type explicitly (e.g., `list[EnsureUnit]` where `EnsureUnit` is a dataclass or NamedTuple with `id: str` and `fn: Callable[[str], EnsureOutcome]` fields) co-located with the `EnsureOutcome` schema in `ensures.py`.
findings.finding: The registry container type is never specified. The plan says 'a registry of ensure units, each {stable, immutable id, callable(tracker: str) -> EnsureOutcome}' but does not say whether this is a `list[dict]`, `list[tuple]`, a `list[EnsureUnit]` dataclass, a module-level `OrderedDict`, or a dedicated `Registry` class. This is a T8(a/b) gap: the data structure schema is referenced throughout (AC1 tests its 'id set', `run_ensures` iterates it) but its structural form is never co-located with the definition.
findings.location: What section / ensures.py (to be created) / AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'The registry is a module-level `list[EnsureUnit]` where `EnsureUnit = dataclasses.dataclass(id: str, fn: Callable[[str], EnsureOutcome])`; order of the list determines execution order and is stable across runs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the ensure-registry pattern: id-stability contract, `EnsureOutcome` semantics, init-time write-lock acquisition rationale, `.ensure-applied` format, and post-rollback behavior.
findings.finding: The ensure-registry introduces a new architectural pattern (desired-state convergence loop, new `EnsureOutcome` contract, new init-time write-lock acquisition) with no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry planned.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'docs' task to the plan: create `docs/adr/NNN-ensure-registry.md` (or a CLAUDE.md section under `_store/`) covering: (1) why ensure-registry over hand-listed calls, (2) id-stability invariant and the frozen-set test as enforcement, (3) lock-acquisition model and LockTimeout no-op rationale, (4) `.ensure-applied` as a hint-only marker (not authoritative), (5) rollback compatibility note.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (with a probe or explicit AC) that `git show tickets:.gitattributes` and `git show tickets:.gitignore` resolve correctly when `tracker` is a symlink path to `main_tracker`, so the idempotency AC ('second run_ensures makes zero commits') holds for the _init_via_symlink paths.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'a second `run_ensures()` on a converged store makes zero changes and zero git commits' (AC line), but `_commit_gitignore`'s migration arm uses `git show tickets:.gitignore` and compares lines as a set — not preserving order. If the committed `.gitignore` has the same lines in a different order, `missing` will be empty and no commit fires. However if `.ensure-applied` is absent (e.g. after rollback) the migration arm re-runs, and if the migration arm appends lines that were already present in a different order it will no-op. This is correct. BUT: the plan never verifies whether `_commit_gitattributes` tree-check (`git show tickets:.gitattributes`) uses the branch-qualified ref `tickets:.gitattributes` or just HEAD — the existing code uses `tickets:` prefix. The ensures unit will run in a context where `HEAD` may point to the tickets branch (inside the tracker worktree). The plan says 'tree-check before committing' as if this is a safe assertion but does not probe whether the `git show tickets:` ref will always resolve inside a symlinked tracker (where the tracker IS a symlink to main_tracker).
findings.location: AC: 'a second run_ensures() on a converged store makes zero changes and zero git commits'; _commit_gitattributes / _commit_gitignore in init.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/scale-envelope.md:69` to the docstring-update scope (AC8): replace the `init._migrate_gc_config` reference with a pointer to the ensure registry.
findings.finding: docs/scale-envelope.md:69 contains a third stale reference to `init._migrate_gc_config` that the plan's docstring-update scope does not enumerate — it only names sync.py:17 and init.py:8 (module docstring). After the migration, this prose link will dangle.
findings.location: What section / 'Update the stale docstring refs' paragraph; AC8
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the 'Update the stale docstring refs' sentence to: '…in `sync.py:17`, the `init.py` module docstring (line 8), AND `docs/scale-envelope.md:69`' and add a corresponding assertion to AC8.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the post-rollback assertion to explicitly cover the `.git/config`-mutating units: state that after rollback to old code, `_migrate_gc_config` and `_ensure_merge_ours_driver` at init.py:210-211 continue to apply `gc.autoDetach=true` and `merge.ours.driver=true` on every re-init — so `.git/config` state is self-healing and no data loss is possible — and confirm `.env-id` is similarly handled by old code's `_ensure_env_id` at line 209.
findings.finding: The plan's post-rollback assertion covers only git-history mutations (`.gitattributes`/`.gitignore` commits) but is silent on the state of the two `.git/config`-mutating units (`gc-config`: `gc.autoDetach=true` / `--unset gc.auto`; `merge-ours driver`: `merge.ours.driver=true`) and the `.env-id` file after rollback. The T11 rubric requires the plan to assert the schema STATE after rollback for every persisted mutation, not just exit codes.
findings.location: What section — 'Post-rollback' paragraph; also AC (implicit rollback coverage)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Post-rollback' paragraph: 'The two `.git/config`-mutating units (`gc-config`, `merge-ours driver`) leave no stranded state: old code still calls `_migrate_gc_config(tracker)` and `_ensure_merge_ours_driver(tracker)` on every re-init (lines 210-211), so any `.git/config` entries written by the new units are either already idempotent or re-applied by old code. `.env-id` is handled by old code's `_ensure_env_id(tracker)` (line 209). No `.git/config` or local-file state requires manual cleanup on rollback.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the test seam for the LockTimeout simulation in AC3, e.g.: 'patch `rebar._store.lock.write_lock.__enter__` (or the lock acquisition call in `run_ensures`) to raise `LockTimeout`, then assert `init_core(...)` returns 0 and emits a warning log.'
findings.finding: AC3 requires a test that asserts init/boot continues (returns 0) when the write lock cannot be acquired, but neither the AC nor the plan body specifies the mechanism for simulating a LockTimeout in the test environment, leaving the executor to guess whether to use mock.patch, a real competing lock, or a subclass — a gap that can produce a test that passes vacuously (e.g., mocking the wrong symbol).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: 'a test asserts init/boot continues (returns 0) when the write lock cannot be acquired'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC3: 'Simulated by patching the `write_lock` context-manager entry in `ensures.run_ensures` to raise `LockTimeout`; assert return code 0 and a logged warning containing "lock" or "sweep skipped".'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `.ensure-applied` has a justified WS1 consumer, or explicitly scope the marker file to WS2 and stub it (e.g. just write an empty list or skip the file) in WS1 to avoid building read-path corruption-recovery logic for a write-only artifact.
findings.finding: `.ensure-applied` is written by WS1 but has zero readers within WS1's own done-definition — it exists entirely for WS2's pending-hint and WS3's fsck/server wiring. The plan's Scope section explicitly lists reading it as Out-of-scope ('pending-hint + .ensure-hinted (WS2), fsck/server wiring (WS3)'). Building a persistent marker file with JSON-parse, atomic-write, and corruption-recovery logic whose only consumer lives in a future workstream is YAGNI within WS1's done-definition.
findings.location: What section ('atomically rewrites `.ensure-applied`...') and AC6 ('`.ensure-applied` written atomically...'); Scope section ('Out: pending-hint + `.ensure-hinted` (WS2), fsck/server wiring (WS3)')
findings.suggested_fix: If WS2/WS3 are planned to ship in close succession, keep as-is and note the forward dependency. If uncertain, defer `.ensure-applied` write to WS2: in WS1, `run_ensures()` returns the outcome list and logs failures, but does not persist any marker file — the marker is introduced together with its first reader in WS2.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
